file_name: Nawaz et al. - 2022 - MSeg-net a melanoma mole segmentation network using CornerNet and fuzzy k-means clustering.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model (CornerNet) for object detection and fuzzy K-means clustering for segmentation to identify and segment melanoma moles from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' and presents original experimental work. It proposes a new deep learning model (MSeg-Net) combining CornerNet and fuzzy K-means clustering, describes the methodology, and reports original quantitative results (e.g., 99.32% and 99.63% accuracy) on the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets.

title: MSeg-Net: A Melanoma Mole Segmentation Network Using CornerNet and Fuzzy K-Means Clustering
journal: Computational and Mathematical Methods in Medicine
brief_summary: This study proposes MSeg-Net, a deep learning framework for melanoma mole segmentation that combines the CornerNet object detection model with fuzzy K-means clustering. The system is designed to detect and segment skin lesions from dermoscopic images, addressing challenges such as noise, blurring, and chrominance variations. The authors evaluated the model's performance using two standard public datasets, ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018. Extensive experiments demonstrated the robustness of the approach, achieving segmentation accuracy values of 99.32% on ISIC-2017 and 99.63% on ISIC-2018. These results indicate that the proposed method effectively handles arbitrary lesion shapes and orientations while outperforming several comparative methods in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on the segmentation of melanoma moles/lesions from dermoscopic images using the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets. The introduction mentions squamous cell carcinoma and basal cell carcinoma as other types of skin cancer, but the experimental work is specifically targeted at melanoma lesion segmentation.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma mole regions (lesions) from dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes MSeg-Net, a framework for melanoma mole segmentation. It uses CornerNet for object detection (localization) and fuzzy K-means clustering for pixel-wise segmentation of the diseased area from the healthy region. Performance is evaluated using segmentation metrics such as accuracy (99.32% on ISIC-2017, 99.63% on ISIC-2018), Jaccard index, and Dice coefficient.
The CornerNet component is used to detect and localize melanoma lesions. The model achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.967 on ISIC-2017 and 0.988 on ISIC-2018 for detection.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2017
datasets_used.description: A standard public database/benchmark for skin lesion analysis, specifically used in the ISIC-2017 challenge.

use.training: 60% of the data was used for model training.
use.validation: 10% of the data was used for validation.
use.testing: 30% of the data was used for model testing.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis against top-performing teams from the ISIC-2017 challenge leaderboard.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2018
datasets_used.description: A standard public database/benchmark for skin lesion analysis, specifically used in the ISIC-2018 challenge.

use.training: 60% of the data was used for model training.
use.validation: 10% of the data was used for validation.
use.testing: 30% of the data was used for model testing.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis against top-performing teams from the ISIC-2018 challenge leaderboard.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Jaccard index, Dice coefficient) and compared against other methods via performance gains and leaderboard rankings.

ISIC-2017.notes: The proposed method achieved a performance gain of 20.91% for Jaccard index, 13.81% for Dice, 6.10% for Accuracy, 2.02% for Specificity, and 17.22% for Sensitivity compared to competitive methods. Average sensitivity of comparative methods was 0.8890.

ISIC-2018.notes: The proposed method achieved a performance gain of 14.20% for Jaccard index, 8.79% for Dice, 5.30% for Accuracy, 3.42% for Specificity, and 7.55% for Sensitivity compared to top teams from the ISIC-2018 challenge leaderboard. Average accuracy of peer teams was 94.33%.

architecture_used.main_model: MSeg-Net (a hybrid framework combining CornerNet for object detection and Fuzzy K-means clustering for segmentation)

architecture_details.backbone: Hourglass model (stacked multiple hourglass modules to capture higher-level information)
architecture_details.detection_mechanism: Estimates top-left (TL) and bottom-right (BR) corners via keypoint calculation
architecture_details.prediction_module: Improved residual block (RB) containing two 3x3 CnL and one 1x1 residual network, followed by a corner pooling layer (CPL)
architecture_details.output_branches: Two separate branches for TL and BR corner estimation producing heatmaps (Hms), embeddings, and offsets
architecture_details.segmentation_component: Fuzzy K-means (FKM) clustering applied to localized moles
architecture_details.preprocessing: Morphological closing operations and unsharp filter

loss_functions: Smooth L1 loss for ground-truth corners
Offset loss (applied only at ground-truth corner locations)

classification_or_output_head.detection_output: Bounding boxes defined by paired keypoints (TL and BR corners)
classification_or_output_head.segmentation_output: Pixel-wise segmentation mask via FKM clustering

preprocessing_techniques: Morphological closing operations to remove unwanted details/artifacts (e.g., hair, tiny blood vessels) using a structuring kernel with squared shape, size 10, and angles 90° and 180°
Unsharp filter application to enrich graphic details and improve visual appearance of skin cancer samples
conclusions: The proposed MSeg-Net framework, which combines CornerNet for object detection and fuzzy K-means clustering for segmentation, effectively handles challenges such as noise, blurring, and chrominance variations in dermoscopic images.
The model is capable of detecting and segmenting melanoma moles of arbitrary shapes and orientations.
The proposed method achieved a segmentation accuracy of 99.32% on the ISIC-2017 dataset and 99.63% on the ISIC-2018 dataset.
MSeg-Net outperformed comparative methods and top teams from the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 challenge leaderboards in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, Jaccard index, and Dice coefficient.
The framework reduces model training and testing time complexity and mitigates overfitting.
The study confirms the robustness of the proposed approach for melanoma lesion segmentation through extensive evaluation on two standard public databases.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on two public datasets (ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018) with a fixed 60/10/30 train/validation/test split; no external validation on independent clinical cohorts was performed, limiting generalizability assessment.

The authors acknowledge a limitation regarding 'small performance degradation' for images with extensive color variations and plan to address this in future work, indicating potential sensitivity to chrominance artifacts.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance comparisons against baseline methods are presented as descriptive gains without statistical significance testing.

The paper does not report the total number of images or class distribution (per-class counts) for the ISIC-2017 and ISIC-2018 datasets used, preventing assessment of class imbalance or dataset size adequacy.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; while the split is defined, the paper does not detail whether preprocessing (morphological closing, unsharp filter) was applied before or after splitting, which could impact validation integrity if applied globally.
The model's complexity and computational efficiency are claimed to be advantages over 'very deep networks,' but specific metrics for inference time, FLOPs, or hardware requirements are not provided.

The study focuses on segmentation and detection of melanoma moles but does not evaluate the downstream clinical utility or diagnostic accuracy for malignancy classification (benign vs. malignant) beyond the segmentation task.
